text: The surgeon uses a cautery scissor in the right hand and a fenestrated Maryland bipolar in the left hand, while the ProGrasp is operated by the fourth arm. The ROSI catheter, a robotic operated suction irrigation device, is visible and used for suctioning during the procedure.
The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg, with the camera port inserted at the umbilicus, providing a view of the surgical field.

The surgical setup includes a scissor port positioned in the right lower quadrant at the umbilical level, alongside an eight millimeter air seal assistant port placed between the scissor arm on the right side and the camera port.

The eight millimeter air seal port is used for insufflation, and the assistant utilizes it to hand needles and place the specimen bag. The surgeon performs suctioning independently using the ROSI catheter.

The surgeon releases the bowel to prevent injury during instrument changes and to avoid inadvertent burning. The bladder is dropped, and the colon is carefully released. The seminal vesicles are approached through a posterior dissection.

The surgeon makes an initial incision approximately one centimeter above the distinct line between the whitish peritoneal reflection on the bladder and the yellowish perirectal fat. The yellow fat is visible at the bottom of the screen, while the white peritoneal reflection is seen at the top. The incision reveals the seminal vesicles and vas deferens.

The surgeon approaches the seminal vesicles and vasa deferentia from a flat orientation, with the vasa visible in the midline extending laterally, and the seminal vesicles positioned below, closer to the rectum.
The surgeon lifts the vas deferens using the fourth arm, revealing the seminal vesicle located below it, closer to the rectum. The medial dissection of these structures is performed.

After completing the medial dissection, the surgeon uses the fourth arm to access the contralateral shoulder, revealing the lateral aspect of the seminal vesicle. The tip of the seminal vesicle is lifted straight up to ensure it is free before proceeding with this maneuver.

The surgeon lifts the seminal vesicle (SV) straight up, then maneuvers around its tip to access the contralateral shoulder, revealing the lateral aspect. The surgeon periodically clutches and pulls further on the SV, adjusting retraction with the fourth arm to ensure the SV is gradually drawn outwards.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the lateral aspect of the seminal vesicle (SV), closely hugging the structure while using bipolar cautery for precise tissue separation.
The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to carefully peel tissue away from the top of the seminal vesicle (SV), ensuring not to veer too wide on the lateral aspect to avoid damaging nearby nerves.

The surgeon carefully dissects the seminal vesicles, ensuring no residual tissue remains on them. The bipolar device is used directly on the seminal vesicles to minimize thermal spread and avoid nerve injury.

The surgeon uses a low cautery setting of twenty-five to minimize thermal spread and potential nerve injury while dissecting around the seminal vesicle. The dissection focuses on hugging the seminal vesicle closely, especially on the lateral aspect where nerves are present. On the medial aspect, where nerves are absent, the surgeon proceeds without concern, and similarly between the seminal vesicles and the prostate on the anterior top side.
The surgeon exposes the vas deferens from the posterior side, revealing the seminal vesicles in a different orientation compared to the traditional anterior approach.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to lift the vas deferens straight up, freeing the medial aspect of the seminal vesicle. The dissection progresses towards the tip of the seminal vesicle, and then shifts to the contralateral shoulder to expose the lateral aspect. The fourth arm stabilizes the seminal vesicle to prevent movement during dissection.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robotic system to provide counter traction and stabilize the seminal vesicle (SV) during dissection. On the medial aspect, cautery is used carefully, but as the dissection reaches the tip and lateral aspect, the surgeon closely hugs the seminal vesicle to avoid nerve injury. Vessels are peeled off the seminal vesicle rather than cauterizing them from a distance. The fourth arm is actively adjusted to maintain optimal exposure throughout the procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects towards the tip of the seminal vesicle, staying close to its surface to avoid nerve damage. Vessels over the seminal vesicle are taken and peeled away using bipolar energy, with the cautery set at twenty-five to minimize thermal spread.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the tip of the seminal vesicle, transitioning to a position that provides a view into the contralateral shoulder to expose the lateral aspect. The dissection is performed close to the seminal vesicle to preserve the nerves running along its lateral border, minimizing the risk of nerve injury.

The surgeon carefully buzzes and peels vessels off the seminal vesicle, ensuring not to veer off its edge to avoid nerve injury. The fourth arm provides counter traction, stretching the seminal vesicle out of the posterior peritoneum hole for better exposure. The vas is visible, and the surgeon waits to cut it until reaching the lateral aspect of the seminal vesicle.

The surgeon carefully cuts the vas deferens along the edge of the dissected seminal vesicle, ensuring both ends retract away from the lateral edge of the SV. The dissection continues upwards towards the prostate, aiming to mobilize the tip of the SV for easier manipulation once the posterior bladder neck is accessed.

The surgeon mobilizes the tip of the seminal vesicle (SV) and some lateral tissue to facilitate lifting once the bladder is accessed. The posterior leaflet of Denonvilliers' fascia is visible, with the incision made approximately one centimeter above the yellow periorectal fat to avoid rectal injury. The incision is kept narrow to prevent ureteral injury, ensuring the ureters remain unseen.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the seminal vesicles, ensuring the incision is made one centimeter above the reflection to avoid the ureter. The bladder is then dropped to enter the space of Retzius.

The surgeon describes making an inverted U incision at the dome, following it down the midline to the bone. Alternatively, the incision is made to the left and right of the ligaments first, locating the pubic bone before completing the midline incision, ensuring the dome is dropped last. This technique provides landmarks to avoid accidental entry into the bladder or overshooting the pubic bone.

The surgeon makes an incision to the right of the ligament on the right side, carrying it down until the pubic bone is visible. A natural plane is identified, allowing for blunt dissection, ensuring minimal perivascular fat is left on the node packet.

The surgeon identifies a natural plane with a cobweb-like appearance during the node dissection, making an incision at the lateral edge of the ligament. This plane allows separation of perivascular fat from the lateral sidewall without cutting through excessive fat, even in obese patients.

The surgeon develops a surgical plane, encountering a slightly stuck area in the corner due to a small hernia. The bladder has been dropped, making it intraperitoneal, which exposes the hernia defect to the bowel. The surgeon identifies the hernia sac and considers repairing it to prevent potential bowel obstruction.

The surgeon makes incisions on both sides of the ligaments, exposing the pubic bone. The visual confirmation of the pubic bone ensures precise alignment, preventing accidental entry into the rectus muscle or epigastric vessels, and avoiding posterior entry into the dorsal venous complex.

The surgeon continues dissection within the cobweb-like plane, using blunt dissection and cautery to manage small bleeders. The bladder is mobilized and dropped, revealing a hernia where bladder fat protrudes. The surgeon identifies the plane between the nodes and perivascular fat, ensuring the fat is not left on the sidewall to avoid interference with node dissection.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to address an adherent area, revealing prominent lymph nodes on the left side. Fat is observed protruding into the obturator canal, indicating an obturator hernia. The surgeon carefully reduces or divides the herniated fat to enhance mobility towards the prostate base, ensuring no bleeding occurs that could retract into the canal.

The surgeon cleans up the fat on the prostate, exposing the superficial venous complex. The fat is sometimes sent as a specimen due to the presence of nodes, although it is rare for these nodes to contain cancer.
The surgeon uses electrocautery set at forty-forty to remove perivascular fat obscuring the view of the bladder neck, ensuring a clear field for the upcoming bladder neck dissection and node removal.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robotic system for retraction and incises the endopelvic fascia to better identify the bladder neck for dissection. The fascia is incised to reveal the contour of the prostate at its base, facilitating the upcoming prostatectomy.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to retract the bladder medially, revealing the plane between the perivascular fat and the nodes. The obturator nerve is visible, emerging clearly in this relatively thin patient. The surgical field shows minimal fat surrounding the obturator nerve and vein, with most of the tissue being the node packet.

The surgeon makes an incision over the vein, carefully dissecting tissue directly on top of it to better define anatomical planes. Bipolar energy is used extensively to manage smaller lymphatics, avoiding the use of clips in this area. Clips are applied distal to the node of Cloquet and proximal near the obturator nerve to prevent back bleeding of lymphatic fluid.

The surgeon uses bipolar energy to seal lymphatics and minimize lymphatic leaks, applying clips on lymphatics distal to the node of Cloquet to prevent leakage from the leg. A clip is placed on the proximal end of the lymphatic packet as the procedure progresses.

The surgeon carefully uses bipolar energy to seal lymphatics and applies clips where necessary to prevent lymphoceles during the prostatectomy. The surgical field shows meticulous attention to sealing lymphatics around the node packet, avoiding monopolar cautery to reduce the risk of asymptomatic lymphoceles.

The surgeon performs an anatomic dissection of the lymph node packet, using bipolar energy to seal lymphatics around the packet. The dissection occurs between the muscle of the pelvic sidewall and the vein, with the obturator nerve visible in the field. The surgeon carefully removes a zone of tissue, ensuring the nodal basin is cleaned out without leaving clumps of fat or nodes.

The surgeon performs a limited node dissection, focusing on the iliac and obturator nodes, and occasionally targeting the internal iliac nodes. The dissection avoids extending towards the common iliacs, which is reserved for higher-risk prostate cancer cases. The surgical field shows careful manipulation around the obturator nerve and the use of bipolar energy to seal lymphatics, minimizing the risk of lymphoceles.

The surgeon places a second clip on the lymphatics to prevent backflow from the node packet being removed. Both the proximal and distal ends are clipped, and bipolar cautery is applied to seal the lymphatics in between, minimizing the risk of lymphocele formation.

The surgeon uses cautery set at forty to manage thermal spread while dissecting deeper nodes beyond the obturator nerve. On the medial side, the internal artery is visible, and some internal iliac nodes are removed from the deeper medial aspect near the obturator nerve.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robotic system to retract the bladder medially, pulling it to the right side. This maneuver exposes the internal iliac vessels and allows for deeper dissection beyond the obturator nerve on the downside. The surgical field is cleared of excess fat tissue to facilitate access to the internal iliac nodes.
The surgeon uses a rosy catheter to suction fatty fluid from the surgical field, improving visibility and contact with the cautery device. This step is part of the ongoing node dissection process.

The surgeon inspects the area beneath the vein to ensure no lymphatic vessels are left behind after the dissection. A clip is visible on the dissected area to prevent lymphatic leakage. The surgeon then prepares to perform a similar dissection on the opposite side before proceeding to the prostate.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to retract the bladder to the right side of the screen, exposing the surgical field. The initial step involves identifying the vein, while removing overlying adipose tissue that could obstruct the view. The surgeon carefully dissects and removes the fat, which contains visible lymphatic vessels, to prevent postoperative leakage. The vein is exposed, and the surgeon performs an anatomical dissection by closely following the vein's wall and peeling away surrounding tissue.

The surgeon identifies anatomical structures and carefully dissects around them to remove the entire lymph node packet in one piece, maintaining anatomic integrity. The technique involves hugging the structures closely to avoid separating nodes into individual chunks, ensuring a clean and organized dissection.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to carefully seal the epigastric vessels, which are visible branching off the iliac vessels and running anterior to the abdominal wall. The camera view is slightly obscured due to a dirty lens.

The surgeon has defined the vein and cleaned off its anterior surface. The assistant switches out the clip, and the surgeon uses a robotic clip applier to place a Hemalox clip at the corner near the note of Cloquet. The robotic clip applier allows precise placement due to its wristed design, which is crucial for clipping the pedicles of the prostate without injuring nerves or leaving prostate tissue behind.

The surgeon uses a robotic wristed instrument to place clips on the anterior surface of the vein, proceeding to dissect the underside of the vein all the way to the pelvic sidewall. The vein is retracted to ensure a well-defined anatomic dissection, with care taken to avoid leaving excess fat on the underside.

The surgeon uses a scissor instrument to retract the vein, ensuring no damage is done to the vein itself. In the past, instrument covers on scissors sometimes failed, causing burn holes, but this issue has been resolved. If a small hole or tear occurs in the vein, a robotic Hemalox clip is applied tangentially to repair it without constricting the vein.

The surgeon completes dissection of the medial aspect of the vein and approaches the obturator nerve, visible in the surgical field. Using bipolar cautery, the surgeon minimizes electrical current through the obturator nerve to prevent involuntary movement. The obturator artery is identified, running into the obturator canal, and may be sacrificed if necessary. A clip is placed on the proximal end of the lymph node packet near the corner where the nerve meets the iliac vein, with ongoing dissection to thin out the area.

The surgeon carefully dissects and thins out a prominent lymph node to avoid clipping it, ensuring complete removal. The obturator artery is visible, and the obturator nerve is defined to prevent accidental clipping, which could lead to temporary leg adduction palsy. The surgeon uses precise clipping techniques to avoid nerve damage.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic instrument to thin out the tissue at the corner near the nerve, preparing to place a clip while carefully identifying the nerve to avoid clipping it. Lymphatics on the medial side are separated to ensure they are not included in the clip.

The surgeon clips the other side, moving deeper to the nerve to address internal nodes on the deep aspect. The nerve is identified, and the node packet is positioned beyond any nodes in the area. The surgical field becomes fluid-filled, affecting the bipolar instrument's efficiency, prompting the potential use of suction. The surgeon continues working deep to the nerve, removing nodes potentially involved in prostate cancer.

The surgeon has completed dissection of the internal iliac nodes, revealing the internal iliac artery. There is visible bleeding in the corner, which is managed using a suction instrument. The surgeon applies additional bipolar cautery to control bleeding, noting the absence of a clip placement in this instance.

The surgeon uses a robotic suction device to manage bleeding in difficult-to-reach corners of the surgical field. The device is applied directly to the bleeding site to hold pressure and stop the bleeding, then slowly retracted to reveal the source of the bleeding.

The surgeon completes the node dissection and removes the anterior prostate fat, placing it in a bag to be set aside until the end of the procedure. The surgical team prepares to clean the scope for improved visibility before proceeding to the bladder neck dissection. The scope cleaning is noted as the first of the case, facilitated by using the XI robot's two X magnification to reduce fogging and splatter.

The surgeon reduces the cautery setting to 25 to minimize tissue charring while dissecting the bladder neck. The incision reveals mostly white tissue, indicating the longitudinal bladder neck muscle fibers. The surgeon carefully avoids cutting into the prostate, which would appear as spongy, marshmallow-like tissue. The incision is made over the prostate, with backward pressure applied to separate the bladder from the anterior prostate, following its contour to locate the urethra in the midline.

The surgeon carefully dissects the sides of the prostate, using the contour to ensure correct positioning. The cautery is set low to visualize muscle fibers, with the left hand providing counter traction. The surgeon avoids digging into tissue, instead lightly touching with the cautery to allow muscle fibers to split naturally. The urethra is lifted with the fourth arm to identify the posterior bladder neck. The incision begins at the corners to maintain a tight bladder neck, avoiding a large opening by not starting at the six o'clock position.

The surgeon carefully cuts the corners of the bladder neck, bringing them towards the center to maintain a tight 'O' shape. The six o'clock position is addressed last, ensuring the bladder neck size is fixed. The left hand stretches the tissue while the monopolar cautery gently touches the longitudinal muscle fibers, causing them to split with minimal charring. The posterior bladder neck has been released, and the surgeon confidently avoids entering the prostate.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to lift the median lobe and releases the corners from either side, ensuring the bladder neck remains tight. The left hand provides stretch, revealing the bladder neck fibers and confirming the dissection is away from the prostate. The seminal vesicles, previously dissected, are visible, indicating the correct location through the posterior bladder neck.

The surgeon completes the posterior bladder neck dissection, revealing the vas deferens and seminal vesicles. The dissection proceeds by removing bladder muscle fibers from the lateral aspects of the prostate base, exposing perivascular fat and vessels. The bladder is fully released and positioned away from the surgical field, allowing clear visualization of the rectum without the need for retraction.

The surgeon performs rectal dissection by making an incision in the posterior Denonvilliers fascia, entering the perirectal fat plane, and peeling the perirectal fat off the backside of the prostate while leaving a leaflet of Denonvilliers fascia on the prostate. This approach creates a mostly blunt dissection plane, similar to the technique used in open prostatectomy.

The surgeon performs a high anterior release of the prostatic fascia, exposing the neurovascular bundle along the prostate. The incision is made high up to identify the plane and follow it down to where the main trunk of the neurovascular bundle resides. Veins are visible in the area, and the procedure is conducted at a pneumo pressure of six millimeters of mercury, minimizing venous bleeding.

The surgeon uses a robotic clip applier to place a clip on the pedicle close to the prostate, ensuring it is positioned high to avoid injuring the neurovascular bundle. The neurovascular bundle is visible at the six o'clock position on the screen, turning and moving posterolateral to the prostate. The pedicle is clipped and divided with minimal tissue left at the base of the prostate.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection on the posterolateral aspect of the prostate gland, carefully releasing the neurovascular bundle. A small incision is made at the lateral edge of the Denonvilliers fascia to facilitate this release, leaving a square of fascia on the posterior side of the prostate. The dissection is mostly blunt, with occasional snipping required to cut through the fascia. The air seal system maintains constant pneumo pressure, minimizing venous bleeding while the suction catheter continuously removes smoke from the surgical field.

The surgeon places a single clip at the base of the prostate capsule, ensuring it is close to the prostate to maximize nerve sparing. The clip is positioned precisely to avoid leaving excess tissue at the pedicle, maintaining the integrity of the neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon is positioned at the prostate capsule, preparing to cut the medial edge of Denonvilliers' fascia to free the neurovascular bundle. Blunt dissection is used to peel the neurovascular bundle off the prostate, facilitated by a high anterior release on the gland's lateral aspect. The pedicle was taken at the correct location, allowing entry into the appropriate plane for nerve sparing. The medial side requires cutting the fascia edge, while the rest involves blunt dissection. The neurovascular bundle dissection extends to the apex of the gland to prevent accidental injury during subsequent steps.

The surgeon cuts through the dorsal venous complex (DVC) without pre-placing a stitch or using a stapler, opting to over-sew it afterwards. This approach allows flexibility in cutting the DVC closer or further from the prostate based on the risk level of the case. Despite the low pneumo pressure of six, the venous bleeding observed is manageable, with arteries being controlled using bipolar cautery.

The surgeon cuts the urethra, revealing the catheter, and pulls it back to expose the posterior aspect of the urethra. The prostatectomy is completed after cutting through the urethra, followed by an instrument change to needle drivers for over-sewing the DVC. The prostatectomy specimen shows effective nerve sparing, with minimal tissue left at the base of the prostate near the seminal vesicles.

The surgeon places a figure-of-eight stitch using an ovicryl suture and a CT-one needle to secure the dorsal venous complex (DVC), ensuring the stitch remains medial to the neurovascular bundle. The procedure involves tying the knot and potentially adding additional throws if the DVC is larger than can be encompassed by a single stitch. Despite some venous bleeding, the robotic magnification shows a pool of blood that appears larger than it is, with typical blood loss remaining low and visibility adequate for precise cutting.

The surgeon places additional sutures to incorporate the remaining dorsal venous complex (DVC) into the stitch, noting that bleeding has ceased. The puboprostatic ligaments are visible and included in the stitch. The DVC stitch is then anchored through the pubic symphysis to compress the DVC against the bone, potentially aiding in postoperative bleeding control. The surgeon uses a CT-one needle with ovicryl suture, cut to eleven inches, for the Rocco stitch or posterior reconstruction.

The surgeon uses a rosy catheter to suction blood clots for improved visibility during the posterior reconstruction and anastomosis. The ovacral suture is prepared for the reconstruction, staying medial to the neurovascular bundle. The suture is placed horizontally under the urethra, incorporating the posterior plate and cut edge of Denonvilliers' fascia, along with the peritoneal edge near the seminal vesicles.

The surgeon incorporates the edge of the peritoneum into the stitch to close the incision made for accessing the seminal vesicles, preventing any structures from moving behind the bladder. The posterior bladder is pulled down away from the bladder neck to avoid interference with the anastomosis. A slider knot is used to secure the stitch, reducing tension on the anastomosis and preventing bleeding from small arteries and venous sinuses behind the urethra. The Stratafix barbed suture is used for the anastomosis, with a Pledge-It to prevent the suture from pulling through on the bladder side.

The surgeon begins the urethral anastomosis by placing the first stitch at the four or five o'clock position, inserting two stitches through the urethra and three through the bladder, then cinching them down to complete the posterior aspect of the anastomosis. The posterior wall is approximated mucosa to mucosa, ensuring no leaks will occur in this area.

The surgeon uses their left hand to identify the urethral edge and places a suture through the mucosa without the aid of a catheter. The suture is advanced up to the ten or eleven o'clock position, avoiding the twelve o'clock position to facilitate the anterior anastomosis. The surgeon uses a suction catheter to clear the field, ensuring visibility of the mucosal edges on both the urethra and bladder for precise mucosal apposition, which is crucial to prevent postoperative leaks.

The surgeon completes the anterior portion of the anastomosis, ensuring mucosa-to-mucosa approximation to prevent urine leaks and inflammation. Barbed sutures are used to achieve a watertight seal, and the bladder is filled with saline to test for leaks. No JP drain is left due to the confidence in the anastomosis integrity.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis and inserts a new 18 French Foley catheter. The bladder is filled with saline to test the anastomosis, confirming no urine leak. The needles are removed, and the prostate is bagged, concluding the procedure. The patient is prepared for postoperative observation, with plans for same-day discharge.
